text: Manhunt 1-2-3. *A game that was actually banned in my primary school due to people having a weird idea that it was connected to some murder... thing. Was weird. * *Basically, it was a game of tic, or as Americans call it, tag, except that anyone you tagged became a Hunter. To make it balanced, you had to hold your finger on someone and say'Manhunt, 1, 2, 3' clearly and loudly. If they squirmed away then the chase had to continue. In reality, what typically happened is you had one really motivated initial Hunter, but the people he turned tended to be pretty lazy about it. However, it was a fun concept, and me and some friends at the time did expand the rules to include'safe zones' and'neutral areas'. I've always felt it could make a good Augmented Reality game if expanded, sort of like a virtual version of the Mirror's Edge setting* -- - The Hunters were everywhere. Of that much, Jack could be certain. However, most typically kept to the rooftops, to get a better view. author: human
text: I am a cruel human. I have committed unspeakable acts against all that is pure and if I should die for it, then I accept my fate. For there is no undoing of the things I've done. A door shuts behind me. I'm in a different room now and I've been sat in a chair that is cold to the touch. I shiver alone, sitting there in silence and reflecting on what has brought me to this chair. Man, and woman, live by rules. Rules, whether they be religious, intrinsic, natural, or self-imposed, govern our lives. I lived and still live by some combination of the aforementioned. But, my will is not always strong. We endure external pressures that some can absorb but others can not, like a home absorbing storm after storm, they begin to break down and weather -- -some sooner than others. Our restraint and control is not much different. I do n't know if my control is predisposed to weakness, is there some flaw that is unknown to me and only needed substrate for a reaction? I feel remorse and as my fingers clasp the cold arms of the chair it is not them that reach into my soul and make me shiver again but the guilt I bear. The door opens and closes behind me

text: `` It's not your fault. You could n't have known.'' Those were the last words of the woman I called my enemy. The last words she said before she pulled the trigger, staining her red hair a deeper shade of red. I stared out of the window of the highrise building that held her office. Outside the building the fires burned, the people screamed, and the world called my name. How could I tell them that the same people that gave me my Suit were the ones that caused this destruction? I took my helmet off and dropped it to the floor, the heat was too much to handle. Her particular brand of anarchy could have saved us from this genocide, this extinction, if only I had n't stopped her. I laugh at the irony, louder and louder until the tears flow down my face. Everything is wrong. I ca n't get her last words out of my head. The last bit of kindness from a woman I wrote off as evil. I wonder if she knew what she would be doing to my mind when she said that. text: It had been just another night, Jack remembered. He had a particularly good time playing Dota 2 with several friends he met recently through the game. Shutting off his computer, he stood up, tired from a long day, to perform his usual bedtime routine. Looking in the mirror while brushing his teeth, he could n't help but think about how he had been a disappointment. How he lost his only girlfriend and prospective wife to drugs and lies. He had been sober for a year now, but it did n't make him feel any more alive. He stood there, toothbrush unmoving in his mouth, lamenting his nonexistent social life and lack of female interaction. `` If only I could meet a nice girl somewhere, I'd be doing alright'' he thought. His work consumed him, and he knew it. Working for an oil company had him traveling to remote places for long periods of time, and tonight was a rare evening home in his cozy apartment on the outskirts of Denver. text: Your message was shocking for all of us, it used any taught plane as a speaker so that every window, metal panel and strung wire had screamed it out, we noted after international communications had been restored that it came in the form of the local language everywhere, simply figuring out how that was done had been the subject of much debate for years afterwards. Now that we knew there was something to look for we spotted some, maybe all, of your technology that drifted round the Sol system. When we pinpointed the broadcast location in Mercury, so long thought to be a planet, now turned out to be a solar power plant, we discovered the directed energy beams that pulsed off it with mathematical regularity those allowed us to find the rest. The Kuiper belt turned out to have millions of drone satellites circling in it, whether the satellites or the belt came first has not been determined, but that it was one of these that killed Voyager was not in debate. Spectroscopic analysis of the beam that killed our first extra solar''ship'' showed us whole new vistas of wavelengths and frequencies and immediately both regressed and massively advanced our understanding of the universe. text: She wakes up in stages. The light in the cottage bends along the curves of her shoulders and I wonder if there could ever be anything in the world as soft as this. The door to the patio is open and spring breezes carry in the smells of a world being reclaimed. Whatever I was thinking about is long gone. She makes a how-about-coffee kind of noise while stretching out across the bedsheets. “ Sure. Same as normal, or something different? ” “ Why? ” “ Well... because things are different today. You know? ” I get up and consider putting clothes on as I talk. “ This is the beginning of a whole new life for us. ” “ I think— ” she clears her throat, “ that it's going to be a whole new life with the same milk, no sugar. ” “ Hmm. ” I'll stay naked. “ I can see this is n't going to work out then. ” She rolls over and drops her face into the pillow. text: Initially, I did n't think much of it. It was my first night visiting my parents' home in over a year, and I was n't too familiar with the layout of the house. I figured it was my mother in the other room, probably clearing her throat as she tried to fall asleep. But when I heard it again, I remembered: *My parents are n't home yet. * They had left earlier this evening to go see a play in the Opera House. I glanced over at the clock. It was n't even midnight yet. A faint chill ran down my spine. If that was n't my mother coughing -- then who was? Maybe it was just a squeaky pipe or something. I crept out of the guest bedroom and walked down the hall toward the adjacent room. I put my ear up against the door. *Nothing. * Maybe it was my imagination? I opened the door and swung it open. Inside was my dad's study, dimly illuminated with an office lamp sitting on his desk. I glanced over at the piles of old papers stacked against the wall, a remnant of when my dad used to work for the city... The sound came again. It was a faint woman's cough, just like before. text: I had about 5 seconds to get used to lying on my back and frankly I was comfortable there until the 6th second rolled around and someone kicked me in the gut. You know what that feels like? A good solid kick in the gut that wakes you right up and rearranges your priorities in a hurry starting with `` make that not happen again, please''. So I was pretty pissed when I opened my eyes, breath gathered ready to swear the first person I saw into oblivion. That it was me made me pause halfway through `` Sonovvua-'' It was me. I'd kicked me. Or at least me with breasts, muscles and glasses, none of which I remembered having. I put my face in my hands, rubbed my eyes with my palms and opened them again to see, yup. Lady Me. At first I though it was some crude creation of some meth tweakers made of dead cats and fish but then this harry, scalie thing turned to me and gave me a grin. Easy there young-en!'' it said in a disturbingly human voice `` Where be you off to in such a hurry''? Naturally I answered it. I spoke back to the `` troll'', I must have been in shock still. `` I was

author: human
text: I'm sitting opposite a man. Someone who I had never met before in person but who's life I have memorized in its totality. My childhood has always been rather hazy, so I feel as though I've supplemented it with pictures of happiness found in the old, meticulous diary entries of the individual before me. It is n't the first time I've done this. The only way to get someone to talk is to truly become the them. Truth is as relative as the universe in which it has been conceived and I for one have created a very relative universe for myself. `` Blink once for a yes and twice for a no, do you understand?'' 'Blink' The white walls of the eight by ten room had recently really started to take a toll on me, but they served a purpose. There was a certain spaceless quality it imbued that allowed me to pull the mark out of the real world, and into one of my own creation. `` Now, I am going to show you a short clip. You will not like the contents of this video. You are to watch the whole thing and will not close your eyes or look away for it's duration. Is this also understood?'' A pair of piercing blue eyes stared back defiantly.

author: human
text: Genetics are such a strange thing, such a bizarre coincidence of DNA and traits. Take me for example. I have a very unique genome. Immutable, my doctor calls it. Apparently that means that even when bitten by a vampire, attacked by a werewolf, or even cursed by magic, I remain human. Stubbornly so, in fact. I'm nearly 1400 years old, yet another strange trait of my immutable genome. My cells replicate perfectly, giving me a theoretical immortality and I do n't really get sick. Like I said, genetics are funny. When I was born, I remember being treated as a freak, someone strange and different. My doctors took a lot of blood and tissue samples, and I remember spending a lot of time with needles in me. Then of course, came the Upheaval. It started about fifty years after I was born, and it continued for nearly three centuries. Mankind slowly went extinct, our place taken by the supernatural creatures of legends. No one really knows how it started, but it began in Europe. Vampires and Werewolves emerged and began killing and turning everyone they could find. Then came the Fomor, the Seelie and Unseelie, and all the other Gloaming creatures. By the end, every human had been turned or killed, except one. I was kept as a science experiment

author: human
text: I kicked off my gym shoes as I walked into my apartment. My entire body ached from the day. What time was it? Late enough to be pitch black outside. God, I could use a whole body massage right now. I fell into the couch and stretched out, feeling each muscle tense up, from my torso to my calves, and then relax. I breathed out audibly. Today ’ s training had been the toughest yet - a relentless cycle of pounding the punching bag, hours in the ring, and shooting drills. Mark, my trainer, had been more determined than ever to train, but I had also sensed his underlying, quiet distress throughout the day. The doorbell rang suddenly, startling me. I got up quietly from the couch and peered through the peephole. There was a woman standing there, with long red hair and thick framed glasses, looking down the hallway. She looked vaguely familiar somehow, but I couldn ’ t place her. I opened the door tentatively. The woman stood in the doorframe staring at me for a long moment, a knot on her forehead, frowning slightly. “ Alexa? Is that really you? Alexa Goodman? ” she had an untraceable accent. “ That ’ s my name. Can I help you with something? ” The woman ’ s face brightened,

text: `` Now we head over to the war-torn sector Y-5 solar system, where two moons of the neutral Narjinn planet were obliterated by a series of Jukoo projectiles, said to have been blown off course by solar wind. Tragedy in-'' *click-* `` -and you're going to take the tail and wrap it three fold around the rest of the body. Now here's the step everyone misses. Come in a little closer, because this is important. Look here. Everyone's afraid to use these quills in the recipe. Nonsense-'' *click-* `` This is earth. One of the most well reserved planets in the galaxy. This is mostly due to a lack of societal advancement. The humans, earth's most advanced species, have at least acquired some forms of tier one technology. But it is a far cry from even the impoverished sector RR-4. Ah, here is a very curious subgroup of the human species, the northern american. It walks along the designated'concrete': a primitive form of infrastructure, composed mainly of water and sand. The human navigates as best he can, narrowly avoiding other humans and occasionally bumping in to one, not unlike the flightless one-eyed Yipps on planet Voranus. It enters a structure through glass panels and engages with another human at an arrangement of wood and marble. A transaction is made. A

text: Now deep down in hell, where the sinners stood Somethin' bad was brewin', it was nothin' good The demon lords were squabblin', down to a man About who'd rule them all, and be the one in command! I'm the first to sin, the giant on which you stand: If anything it should be YOU beneath MY command!'' Then Beelzebub, now, he would n't settle for this He flew into the air on the foulest of mists `` Now you two have the royal claim, that much I know true But I have one question to pose! a quandary for you!' author: human
text: Stela Malsano confounded every epidemiologist, even someone as knowledgeable as Dr. Anna Walker. Most diseases, you see, spread from an initial vector of infection. A man stumbles into a garbage heap with an open sore and picks up a dangerous bacterium which proceeds to multiply in his body, then spreads it around by coughing and vomiting. A woman orders an exotic undercooked fish at a Japanese sushi restaurant harboring pathogens from the ocean and kisses her children at home. Once the responsible anchors itself in the host, it starts to reproduce and eventually breaks free to infect others. The self-catalytic process repeats itself until it either runs out of hosts, is contained, or simply expires. Not Stela Malsano. When it erupted, it did so instantly around the entire globe as though someone had suddenly pulled a lever, flipped a switch, pressed a button. Entire families on the far side of the earth awoke screaming with vivid hallucinations. Chaos ensued on the highways as drivers seized with convulsions and caught in the grip of lifelike trances lost control of their vehicles, often fatally. Dr. Anna, attending a colloquium on multiverse influence at the University of Illinois, bore witness to the entire room suddenly get up and run around screaming with lunatic abandon. Some thought the zombie apocalypse had begun; others were

author: human
text: The quiet squeaky steps of my front porch have seldom been kept up. I am a brick house. My interior looks like someone from the 70's plastered their retro look on me. Baige shag carpet, green and orange wallpaper, I once felt the hustle and bustle of my tenants, but something happened ten years ago that made the first tenants leave. I do n't know if it was something I did or if it was possibly a death in the family.All I know is I was boarded up and left here. I felt at peace, I rested. It felt good to rest those ten years, but recently, some new tenants came in, I do n't remember them knocking. They filled my air ducts with smoke, and I felt the occassional burn singe into my shag carpet. They came 3 months ago. Ever since then I've felt dirty inside, like there was a grit about me. I overheard one of the tenants scream about drugs and getting high, whatever that means. It's 2am, I notice cars lining up on the block, only they are n't regular cars, they are police cruisers. Well this is embarrassing, it looks like they are looking at my lot. It has n't been kept up. I feel naked. Oh, there they are lining up... oh my

text: To put it politely, today has been a long day. There are some other words I would add to that, but the promise of $ 200 dollars is hard to resist. Staying up all night to cram for the two hardest finals of my academic career ( so far ) was bad enough. Running into my ex-girlfriend and her new boyfriend celebrating being done with finals when I tried to run into Quicki ’ s for a snack between finals was bad enough. Getting urgent calls from my mom asking for $ 50 ( that I don ’ t have ) in order to pay her phone bill in the middle of my second final was bad enough. Having all of that happen today, the last day of my stupid bet with Colin, was more than bad enough, but to make matters worse, ten feet from getting to my dorm and a well deserved nap, I stepped in doggie-doo. All of that was bad enough, so when I opened the door to my dorm and heard a crowd of people shout, “ Happy birthday, Timothy! ” I was less than impressed. I stared the group down for a moment, my eyes glancing over their faces trying to see someone I recognized from a class or one of Colin ’ s

author: human
text: **Odyssey of the Edrathi: A'Home' At Last? ** Our homeworld is long dead, and few of us remain. There were many `` flotilla fleets'' like ours, although what has become of them, who can say? Do the people of Ukrub still number in the millions at least, or are the 437,521 survivors in our fleet all that remain of our race? Even with the tetradrives to travel a lightyear in the span of a month, it would take centuries to properly contact the other fleets, and who's to say we would ever receive an answer? Years of rotating individuals between waking work and cryosleep, the prohibition of bearing children except in extreme circumstances, the reclamation of any and all resources onboard our ships, all to keep us going until we found another planet to settle. We found such a world through the telescopes, the closest world brimming with life in our otherwise barren sector, and set up a string of jumps towards that planet. As we drew ever closer, however, we were able to see the clustered lights on the nightside, and learned that we were indeed not alone in this cold unforgiving universe. We had been given new hopes and fears in a single stroke, for while it was a world harbouring an alien intelligence, we knew so little

author: human
text: Sublieutenant G'rakh sighed as he watched the flickering screen. *Eight years at the Academy just to be stuck at this lousy post*, he thought to himself. G'rakh was one of three officers assigned to the scanning station aboard the starship *Lek'vah*. Hours were long and uneventful, especially in this dead region of space. He looked up as someone walked in the room. It was Lieutenant Salak'kor, his immediate superior. `` Nothing to report here, sir'' he said, as Salak'kor lowered himself into the seat beside him. `` Well, I did n't really expect there to be much'' replied Salak'kor. `` There has n't been any life in this system for thousands of *vrakh*.'' G'rakh called up a review of the system on his screen. He leaned forward and skimmed through the article. `` Simple planetary system orbiting an F-class star. Of the eight planets only one, the third planet in, was believed capable of sustaining life. However, we can only speculate as the planet was destroyed some 6,000 *vrakh* ago.'' G'rakh looked back at the scanner screen. Where there should have been a planet instead was an immense debris field. Most of the fragments were small, about the size of an escape pod, but some were very large, several times larger than the *Lek'vah* herself. One or two of the

author: human
text: Where am I? How did I get here? What is going on? Why is there so much blood in the room? I must keep quiet. I have clearly been attacked. I dont know where my attacker is, but I must get out of here before he returns. I open the door to the room I am in and look around. I see a small room behind a one way mirror. There is a guy on the floor curled up in a ball. He has been beaten badly. Someone very sadistic did this to him. I open his door, and he screams at me to leave. I tell him I will be back for him and he starts crying. Clearly he is mentally unstable. I walk over to him and put my arm on him and tell him he will be alright. I must have touched a broken bone or something, because he jumped at my touch and started screaming more. Do n't worry. I promise I will come back for you. I told him. I shut the door behind him. I go up some stairs and open a door. The door on the other side is impossible to see without knowing where the latch is to open it. I feel around and ifnd out how to open the door from

author: human
text: I am alone. The silence is empowering. Nothing to hear. Nobody to talk to. I can finally be alone with my thoughts. Eternity is mine, and mine alone, and in it, I am whole. I am free. Someone tried to take my eternity from me. A man. He liked to talk. He did n't want to be alone. He would talk for hours, stealing my time from me. He said such strange things. He wanted to bring back humanity. He tried to force me to help him. I hit him in the head with a rock. The man finally stopped talking. And now I am alone again.

author: human
text: He sat on the sidewalk, watching people enter and exit the door before him every few minutes. No one really paid him any attention, maybe because he and his coat were covered in dirt. But, that's the way life was when you did n't have a home. Cleanliness did n't matter as much as satiating your hunger, and there was certainly food to be had beyond this door. There did n't appear to be anything special about the door or even the building. It looked similar to the rest of the buildings on this street. But there was something different about it. Most notably, when the door opened, delicious scents filled his nostrils. A couple and their child emerged from the door and walked down the street. The door stayed open for half a second before it started to close again. During that time, he was hit by a tidal wave of scents that sparked his hunger and summoned saliva. Spiced meat, potatoes, greens, oh he could almost taste it! The excitement the smells inspired brought him to his feet. Surely someone would come outside with extra food? Perhaps they would see him and share their bounty? The thought made his mouth water more. He had to get in there. The door had already closed again by now, but he

author: human
text: `` What about Thursday? We could hang out, see a movie, you could just slip in a few compliments. That's all they really add up to, anyway.'' Thor again. He's persistent, and nice enough in person, but he's like the rest of them. `` Ummm, I do n't know, man. I'm already meeting Mithras for breakfast, one of those buddhas for lunch, and I promised Jesus I'd explain the Internet to him.'' He looked crestfallen. `` You must realize what this situation is like for me, right?'' `` Yes, of course, it's very strange for you, being the last human. I know we all just came out if the woodwork when your species left, but we were struck a terrible blow by their departure.'' If heard things like this before. `` Well, you had plenty of notice. The population began emigrating centuries ago!'' `` Yes, and we carried on in secret. That was probably a mistake. From our point of view, it seemed inconceivable that people would really want to leave! Look at Earth! It's beautiful! We had our little cults and it was working well. Once people left, the loneliness hit like a strange hunger. You're all we have left. You shine like a beautiful light. We just want to commune with you. You could hang out with us

author: human
text: `` You know, I did n't think cynicism was an emotion.'' I mention casually, skirting around the emaciated, mottled grey-black humanoid creature sitting cross-legged on my kitchen table. The, erm, Cynic ( I guess? ) pulls its curtain of greasy black hair back with one hand and gestures to the hair-tie on my wrist with his other. I toss it to him and duck behind the table to grab a couple of sodas from the box on the floor. `` Well for most people it's probably not.'' Cynic answers in a voice that sounds eerily like Jack of Blades from the PC version of Fable. `` But since you developed a ridiculous habit of channeling what little negative emotion you have into fuel for your shitty attitude and rapidly declining sense of humor I get to represent pretty much every negative emotion you have: so that's your pain from your few meaningful rejections, your anxiety about your crashing and burning immediately upon entering your adult life, all of that festering contempt and angst from when you were twelve, the last toxic bits of your episode of existential depression from age fourteen and your overall depressing view of existence. I get to represent all of that because cynical bullshit is all you'll listen too.'' I continue to surprise myself by listening

author: human
text: The house was a quaint house, by no means lavish or excessive. It had harboured a grand total of two families in its life, a fine accomplishment by any house's means. The first family gave the house its name, House. They were also the ones who decided House was a cheery fellow, and gave him a new appearance of vibrant yellow to match this endowed persona. His new skin was a stark contrast to the deep, relaxed green of the forest that infringed upon the property. But all good things must come to an end; and too quickly at that, for houses age much slower than people. The family grew from a man and woman to a group of six. House had no idea if this was a large or small family, after all, he was only a house. In time however, the children grew and eventually and inevitably left the nest, leaving once again only the man and woman, now old and wrinkled. House enjoyed watching over and protecting them in their last days. He enjoyed their quiet reminiscence of bygone times, and the soft way in which they spoke to each other. The way they held each other at night to the manner in which they shared meals together was testimony enough of their love for one another.

author: llm
text:
I turned from the bed and slipped out through the door at its far right, letting it click shut behind me. The hallway was narrow and cool, carrying the faint smell of dust and old wood. I walked its length until it opened into a wider space, then turned left and passed the stairs, their banister gleaming softly in the dim light. Ahead, the next door on my left faced the sitting room, and I paused there, looking into it as if someone might be waiting to be recognized.

Then I turned left again and left the bedroom behind. The hallway seemed longer on the return, as though it had stretched itself while I wasn’t paying attention. I turned left and entered the sitting room, where the air was warmer and still. I waited by the chair, one hand resting on its worn back, listening to the quiet settle around me.

At last I turned around and left the room, retracing my steps into the hallway. I passed the painting without stopping, its dark frame holding a face I couldn’t quite trust. Then I turned left and exited the hallway, and turned left once more, coming to a stop in the hush that followed me like a shadow.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom and crossed to the door on the far left, the one that always sticks a little in its frame. Beyond it, the house breathed colder. I moved forward and paused near the top of the stairs, one hand grazing the banister, listening to the soft creak below as if the floor were remembering someone else’s weight.

When I went on, I kept straight ahead and turned into the hallway to the right. The corridor narrowed around me, holding the day in a thin strip of light. I walked until it opened into the sitting area, where the air changed—warmer, faintly scented with dust and old fabric. I stopped just before the small black table beside the couch, close enough to see the glossy surface catch a dim reflection of the lamp, but not close enough to touch it.

Then I walked down the hall again, slower this time, and passed the painting on the left wall. Its colors seemed to shift as I went by, a dark field of brushstrokes that followed me with a patient, almost human attention. At last I went to the nearest end table beside the couch and stood there, one shoulder angled toward the room, as if I had arrived at the edge of some quiet decision.

author: llm
text:
I left my bedroom and stepped into the hall, turning right as the house seemed to exhale around me. The corridor was dim and familiar, the kind of dark that keeps every old sound in its pockets. At the end, I reached the stairs and went down them carefully, one hand grazing the rail, until I stopped on the first landing. From there the house felt layered, as if I were standing between memories.

I turned right and continued down the hallway, where the wallpaper had begun to peel in small curls like dried leaves. The air grew cooler with each step. I descended the next stairs slowly, listening to the soft complaint of wood beneath my feet, and stopped again, letting the silence settle over me.

A potted plant leaned in the corner, its leaves glossy and dark, and I turned right by it into the narrow hallway beyond. The passage tightened around my shoulders, leading me down to the first set of stairs. I went down just far enough to reach the flat area and waited there, still and quiet, as though the house had been expecting me all along.

author: llm
text:
I left the craft room with glue still sweet on my fingers and turned left into the hush of the hall. The stairs leading down yawned beside me, but I kept toward the front door, where the morning pressed pale light against the glass. I slipped into the formal living room, all careful upholstery and too-still air, then through the opening into the dining room. I stopped just before the table, its polished surface holding the room like a mirror, and waited as if someone might call my name from the next room.

When nothing came, I moved on, walking past the stairs that climbed up to the private dark above. I entered the dining room again, this time from the other side, and stood beside the glass dining table. It seemed colder up close, a clear sheet over four silent legs, as if it had been waiting for a hand to disturb it.

I stepped back out, turned left, and went straight until I passed the stairs. Two chairs guarded the way, one on each side, and I threaded between them, then turned right. At last I waited near the glass table with the white chairs, listening to the building settle around me like a held breath.

author: llm
text:
I climbed the first stairwell slowly, my hand skimming the banister as if it could steady the thought I had brought with me. Near the top, I stopped two steps short, the landing still above me and the air already changed—warmer, dust-softened, carrying the faint perfume of old wood and rain-damp coats.

Then I went on, coming up the remaining steps to the landing and turning left as the architecture asked. The next staircase rose in a narrow, elegant line, and I continued upward with the hush of someone entering a memory. At the top, I paused and looked up at the chandelier suspended there, beautiful beyond reason: a burst of glass and candlelight, each crystal catching the day and breaking it into small, trembling stars. It seemed to float rather than hang, as if the ceiling had opened to let a piece of winter sky inside.

I turned around then, retracing my steps, and walked up the stairs again, the same boards creaking under my shoes, the same light falling in pale strips along the wall. At the top, I waited, breathing quietly, as though the house itself might speak if I stood still long enough.

author: llm
text:
I faced the long kitchen walkway, the counter and its bar stools lined up to my right like patient witnesses, while the large windows on the other side held the afternoon in pale squares. I moved past them, the floor cool under my steps, drawn toward the large dining table ahead as if it had been waiting all day for someone to cross the room. The kitchen smells thinned behind me, replaced by the hush of a more formal space, where even the light seemed to lower its voice.

I kept straight through the kitchen area and beyond the bar stools until the formal dining table came into view, set with the kind of order that suggests no one has eaten there in weeks. From there I entered the room where the sofa rested on my right, its cushions slightly indented, as if memory had sat there and risen a moment before I arrived. I stopped beside the glass table, its surface catching a sharp reflection of the window light.

The room felt paused, the way houses do when they are listening. I stood by the glass table and waited, looking toward the doorway to the right of the dining room table, as though someone important might appear there and explain why the air had gone so still.

author: llm
text:
Steam clung to my skin as I stepped out of the shower and reached for the door to the right of the sink. The bathroom was all wet tile and dim mirrors, the kind that made my own face look like a memory. I turned left, then left again at the pool table, its green felt dim under the low light, a chalky island in the middle of the room. I paused in the bedroom doorway and listened to the house breathing around me, the distant hum of an old machine somewhere deeper inside.

Then I went back out, leaving the bathroom behind me. I turned left, then left again past the pool table, the cue rack casting thin shadows like stripped reeds. The bedroom waited ahead, half-open and unmade, and I passed through the doorway into it, stopping there as if the room had called my name and I had answered too quietly to hear.

Later, I came out of the bathroom once more and passed the chairs lined against the wall, their legs splayed like patient insects. I walked toward the arcade glow, all blinking color and muffled music, and stopped again in the bedroom doorway, caught between the bright noise behind me and the stillness ahead.

author: llm
text:
I stepped out of the closet with the stale smell of cedar clinging to my sleeves and crossed the hall, keeping left of the animal pictures that watched me in their gilt frames. Their painted eyes seemed to follow as I passed the little sculpture on the table, a pale figure caught forever mid-gesture. Beyond it, the hallway opened like a held breath, and I moved into it, drawn toward the bedroom beside the chair. The chair stood angled as if someone had just risen from it, and I paused there, hand on the doorframe, listening to the house settle.

Then I turned left and slipped back into the closet, the dark pressing close around my shoulders. I came out again, turned right, and left the room behind me. A slight turn carried me into the hallway straight ahead, where the floorboards gave a soft complaint under my feet. I continued on, passing the chair on my right, and entered the room beyond. I stopped at the entrance, reluctant to disturb the hush.

At last I turned left once more, left the closet behind, crossed the hall to the left, and entered the next room. I walked down that hall, feeling the air cool and thinner with each step, and found the bedroom at the end waiting for me like a memory I had almost remembered.

author: llm
text:
I climbed the stairs slowly, one hand skimming the wall until the banister met my palm at the landing. At the top I turned and followed the railing into the bedroom area, where the air felt dimmer, hushed by curtains and the weight of sleep. The closet stood ahead like a closed thought, and I walked toward it, hearing the soft creak of the floorboards under my shoes.

Then I went up the stairs again, as if the house had forgotten me and I had to prove my way back. At the top I turned around and slipped through the door past the stairs. The bed waited in the middle of the room, made neatly, almost formal. I walked up to it, then turned right and moved into the doorway, pausing there with one shoulder against the frame, listening to the silence gather in the hall.

After that I walked up the stairs once more, turned left, and entered the second open door on the left. The room opened around me in a pale wash of morning. I crossed toward the foot of the bed, then turned right and entered the open door to the bathroom, where the mirror held my face like a question I had finally decided to answer.

author: llm
text:
I slid past the bed without looking at the rumpled imprint in the sheets, as if the room might accuse me if I met its eyes. At the doorway I turned hard right, the movement abrupt enough to make my shoulder brush the frame, and the bathroom opened in a wash of pale tile and steam. I stopped by the shower, where the glass held a faint fog from someone else’s haste, and the chrome handle was cold under my fingers.

I circled back then, slower, threading around the bed as though it were an obstacle in a dream, and left the bedroom by the same narrow mouth of the door. The first right led me into the bathroom again, the air tighter there, scented with soap and damp towels. I came to a halt in front of the shower, facing the drain as if it might answer a question I’d been carrying all morning. Water ticked somewhere in the pipes.

From the doorway on the right of the bedroom, I stepped into the hallway and turned right, the corridor dim and patient, its walls lined with old light. My footsteps softened on the floorboards. I followed the bend into the bathroom and stopped near the sink, where a single cup sat beside the faucet and my reflection waited in the mirror, pale and unsteady, as if it had arrived before me.

author: llm
text:
I turned left and let the rope guide me as if it knew the way better than I did. At its end, I turned right, then followed it left again, skirting the quiet edge of a room that seemed arranged for absence rather than company. When I entered, I stopped just before the table in the middle, where a bowl of pale fruit sat untouched, as if waiting for a hand that had forgotten the appointment.

I stepped onto the red carpet and turned left. The carpet carried me forward beside a roped-off section, its velvet surface dull under the overhead lights. At the rope’s end I turned right, then moved on, the red runner stretching ahead like a narrow promise. I turned left and passed through the double doors, which opened with a soft, practiced sigh. Beyond them I walked forward and waited near the chairs, their legs aligned in patient rows, their emptiness feeling almost ceremonial.

Then I turned left just before the roped-off section with the table and chairs. I walked into the doorway on the right and stood beside the table and chairs there, close enough to hear the faint hum of the building, as if it were breathing around me.

author: llm
text:
I left the room and turned right into the hallway, where the air smelled faintly of dust and copier toner. The corridor ran long and narrow, its pale walls making my footsteps sound smaller than they were. I walked to the end, where the light pooled thinly around the corner, then turned left and stood at the beginning of the next hall, tucked behind the red chair as if it were a piece of stage scenery. It was upholstered in worn crimson fabric, its legs splayed like a tired animal’s. I waited there for a moment, listening to the building settle.

Then I moved through the doorway and took a right. The hallway beyond was straighter, quieter, with a muted hum coming from somewhere deeper inside. I walked straight down it, past blank doors and a strip of scuffed baseboard, then turned left and stopped near the door on the right. Its handle was cool-looking even from where I stood. I hesitated, then kept going.

I turned right again and walked straight toward the windows. Daylight gathered there in a white wash, softening the edges of everything. By the glass doorways, I turned left and waited right there, with the brightness on my face and the rest of the building falling away behind me.

author: llm
text:
I left the room with my hand still warm on the doorknob, and crossed the hall toward the table where the purple chairs stood like four patient bruises around a bowl of bruised pears. I passed them without touching anything, though the air there smelled faintly of dust and tea. The stairs waited beyond, narrow and pale, and I climbed them one careful step at a time, listening to the house settle around me. At the top, the kitchen opened in a hush of stainless steel and morning light, and I stopped there, as if I had arrived too early for some appointment I could not remember.

From the bedroom, I came again, slower this time, carrying the faint weight of sleep in my limbs. I crossed into the kitchen and paused at the top of the second set of steps, where the tile changed color and the banister turned cool under my fingers. Below me, the house seemed to hold its breath.

Then I moved through the living area, past the low lamp and the worn sofa, toward the stairs on the left, behind the purple chairs. Their backs flashed by like closed lips. I climbed once more, and at the top of the stairway I stopped, listening to the quiet thrum of the rooms below, as if the whole house were remembering me.

author: llm
text:
I left the room with my shoulders already tight, as if the building had been waiting for me to decide something. The corridor stretched ahead in a pale ribbon, and I went straight until the corner opened and I turned right.

Three shallow steps lifted me into a brighter level of the house. I moved right again, climbing the small set of stairs that creaked under my weight, then followed the hall as it narrowed and cooled. At the first doorway on the right, I paused only long enough to recognize the dark rectangle beyond it, then turned into it and stopped, letting the hush gather around me like dust.

But the quiet did not hold. I went up once more, taking the short set of steps to the right, and the hallway there seemed borrowed from another life—longer, dimmer, scented faintly of soap and old plaster. I continued down it until the right-hand turn appeared, a simple opening, almost apologetic. I turned into the bathroom.

The room was small and white and unexpectedly kind. The mirror held only my face, tired and traveling, and the sink waited beneath it like a promise of water.

author: llm
text:
I walked past the chair without touching its worn arm, then turned right as if I had decided something and could not bear to explain it. The white chair waited behind the others, pale and patient, and I stopped behind it, close enough to see the faint crack in its backrest. Beyond it, the room opened in a hush of dust and afternoon light.

I passed the table next, its surface crowded with the usual small clutter of a life paused mid-sentence, and turned right again. The floorboards answered under my shoes. I kept going forward, through the long strip of quiet, until the swing came into view, hanging from its frame like an unfinished thought. I stopped near it and let it move once, barely, from the draft I had made.

Then I went around the table, tracing its edge as though circling a memory I wasn’t ready to name. At the green room I turned left, the color of the walls deep and mossy, and everything in me seemed to soften. I stopped at the white chair there, the same color as bone and moonlight, and sat with my hands folded, listening to the building settle around me.

author: llm
text:
I stepped into the building and let the corridor take me forward, straight as a held breath. Two doors slid by on either side, closed and silent, before I turned left into the bedroom. The room was dim with afternoon light, soft around the edges of the bed, as if it had already begun to forget the day.

I moved back into the hallway and walked on, passing the wall paintings one by one: a field of gold, a woman with her face turned away, a child in a red coat. The laundry room sat humming quietly as I went by, all warm soap and folded towels. Near the wooden cabinets, an open door waited like an invitation, and I crossed through it into the bedroom beyond.

From there I kept straight down the hallway again, my footsteps small against the floor. Another painting watched from the wall, its colors deep and still. When I reached the bedroom at the end, I turned left and stopped in the doorway. Inside, a rocking chair rested beside the bed, both of them arranged with the patience of things that had learned to wait. I stayed there, half in the hall and half in the room, listening to the house breathe around me.

author: llm
text:
I skirted the hot tun, its steam brushing my face like a warning, and slipped through the first door beyond it. Inside the house, I turned right into the room with the bed, where the sheets were still rumpled as if someone had just risen. I moved quietly to the window and stopped beside it before the rug, watching the glass hold a pale reflection of my own shoulders.

From there I turned left toward the palm tree, its fronds spread like a patient hand. I passed the second palm tree and turned right, then left again, following the corridor to a door that sighed when I opened it. On the other side I turned right and went straight until the first lamp came into view, its shade warm and steady, a small sun keeping watch.

I walked around the potted plant, careful not to brush its leaves, and turned left. Past the Jacuzzi tub, where the water shivered softly, I turned right and crossed to the left of the pillar into the building. Then I turned right once more and stopped just inside the bedroom, listening to the house settle around me like a held breath.

author: llm
text:
The Christmas trees ahead glowed with a patient, artificial snow, and I walked toward them as if they were keeping time for me. I turned left and slipped out the door on the left, the hall briefly swallowing the scent of pine. Through the door on the right, the house changed its mind and became quieter. A rug led me like a dark ribbon to the top of the stairs, where I turned left and entered the room with the mirror above the desk. My reflection looked startled to find me there.

I turned left again and passed the bed, its blankets folded with military care, then turned left once more and walked out of the room. To the right lay a little hallway, narrow enough to make my shoulders remember themselves. I went to the top of the stairs and waited, listening to the house breathe below me.

After a moment I turned around and exited the room, as if leaving a dream before it could name me. I took a right, then my first left, and stopped in the doorway of the bedroom. From there I could see the bed, the mirror, and the thin corridor behind me, each one holding a different version of my hesitation.

author: llm
text:
I left my room with the careful hush of someone trying not to wake a memory, turned right, and entered the bedroom where afternoon light lay across the floorboards like a thin sheet. I crossed it slowly, passing the painting of the girl with her fixed, knowing eyes. She seemed to watch me leave as if I were the one being framed. Beyond the window, the air looked bright enough to touch.

I went outside and stopped near the chairs on the deck, where the wood held the day’s warmth in its grain. For a moment I stood with my hand on the railing, listening to the soft clatter of something unseen below, then I turned back and left the bedroom behind me.

The next bedroom waited on the right, and I entered it as if I had been expected. I moved through the room and turned right into the glass doorway. One step carried me outside, where the world opened in a sudden wash of wind and light, and I stopped there, balanced between the cool glass behind me and the air ahead.

Then I left that bedroom too, entered the one on the right, and walked out onto the balcony. Once I was outside, I stopped, looking out over the quiet distance as though the building had finally let me go.

author: llm
text:
I stood facing the pool, its surface flat as a held breath, and turned to my right. The walkway carried me along the water’s edge until the building rose ahead and the path narrowed to its end. A gate waited there, pale and unromantic, and I passed through it, stopping just inside as if I had crossed into a quieter version of the afternoon.

From there I circled the building, my footsteps soft against the siding and concrete, until I reached the gate door on the side. I waited there a moment, with one hand resting on the latch, listening to the low hum of pipes and the distant slap of water behind me. The place seemed to be holding its own secrets in the walls.

At last I followed the porch, angling slightly left toward an open door that breathed out a cooler air. Inside, I turned hard left, the motion abrupt enough to make the hallway feel like a decision. A barred door stood there, and I moved through it, the metal cool against my fingers. Beyond it, everything quieted. I stopped at the glass door, where my reflection hovered over the dim room behind me, and for a second I could not tell whether I was arriving or leaving.

author: llm
text:
I crossed to the far side of the dining table, where the wood held the afternoon in a dull, warm sheen. From there I drifted into the kitchen and stopped at the sink, my hands hovering over the basin as if something might rise from the drain with a message. The room smelled faintly of lemon and old coffee. I moved on to the table beside the couch and stood there, listening to the soft, upholstered silence of the living room breathe around me.

Then I walked past the windows, their pale light sliding over my sleeves, and turned right, then left into the kitchen again. At the counter I turned right and followed the familiar line of the floorboards toward the living room, where I waited at the entrance, half in shadow, half in the day.

A moment later I went past the window on my left and left the dining room behind. I entered the kitchen and turned right, passing to the left of the island, its marble cold and patient beside me. I went straight ahead until the walls seemed to narrow their attention, and I stopped between the wall unit and the end table, caught in the small quiet between one room and the next.

author: llm
text:
I stepped out of the room and turned right into the great open area, where the ceiling rose like a held breath. The light there was thin and pale, spread across the floor in long, uncertain sheets. I crossed to the stairs and descended to the landing, each step giving a small hollow answer under my shoes. At the top of the landing I paused, one hand resting on the rail, listening to the building settle around me.

Then I moved into the corridor, where the air felt narrower and warmer, and made a right toward the top of the next stairs. The walls seemed to lean in with the hush of old paper. I went down a few steps and stopped mid-stairs, suspended between floors, with the corridor above and the lower level opening beneath me like a thought I had not finished thinking.

I continued down the first set of stairs, slower now, as if the building were asking me to remember something I had almost lost. At the first landing I stopped again. From there I could hear footsteps somewhere deeper inside, and for a moment I stood still, halfway between arrival and leaving.

author: llm
text:
I left the dining table behind me and skimmed along the granite countertop, its cool edge guiding my hand as I moved through the kitchen. The sink flashed under the light, a small basin of silver where a lemon peel floated like a bright thought. Beyond it, the house opened toward the patio, and I could already feel the change in air waiting outside. I crossed the tile and stopped near the outdoor sofas, their cushions pale and sun-warmed, as if someone had just risen from them and vanished into the afternoon.

Then I turned back, facing the dining room table, and took a left through the kitchen again. The counters stretched beside me like a familiar shoreline, cluttered with a bowl of apples, a dish towel, a knife laid down carefully. I passed into the living room, where the couch sat low and deep behind me, holding the shape of an unspoken conversation. I paused in the doorway to the patio, caught between the house and the open air, listening to the soft hum of the refrigerator and the faint rustle of leaves outside.

At last I went through the kitchen once more and waited in the archway between the two sitting areas, where the light from one room met the shadow of the next, and neither side quite claimed me.

author: llm
text:
I began in the nursery, where the doll hutch stood like a little chapel of glass and painted faces. I turned left, then right, and let the room narrow into the bedroom, where the air smelled faintly of starch and sleep. There I turned left again and slipped into the bathroom, stopping in front of the sink as if I had come to answer a question I had forgotten.

When I moved on, I walked forward to the curio cabinet, its shelves crowded with small bright silences, and turned right. The bedroom opened ahead of me. I crossed to the foot of the bed, where the quilt drooped in a heavy, careful fold, then turned left and passed the leather chair, its arms polished dark by years of waiting. Through the doorway, I found myself near the sink again, the basin pale under the light, as though the house had returned me to the same thought by another corridor.

At last I walked down the hall leading to the cabinet, each step soft against the worn floor. At the cabinet I turned right and entered the bedroom once more. Inside, I took a left and went into the bathroom on the far left. I stopped on the rug in front of the sink, feeling the house settle around me, quiet as held breath.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom with my hand still half-curled around the cold knob, as if I might return before the room noticed. The living room opened in front of me, all soft shadows and the hush of late afternoon, and I walked straight through it without looking at the couch, without letting myself sink into the familiar dip of its cushions. The air changed as I crossed into the dining area. There, I stopped and waited, facing the table that held nothing but a faint ring from last night’s glass and the memory of voices around it.

I stepped out of the bedroom again, this time turning left at the threshold, and headed forward past the living room area. The carpet gave way to the harder, cooler floor near the dining room, where the glass table stood like a held breath. I paused beside it, my reflection ghosted and broken in the surface, my own outline split into pieces by the light.

Then I turned left at the end of the bed and kept walking straight until the bar came into view. Three stools waited there, aligned like patient witnesses. I stopped behind the last one and rested my hands on its back, as if I had arrived somewhere I’d been meaning to go all along.

author: llm
text:
I turned around and went down the hallway, my shoes whispering over the worn floor, the air cooler the farther I went from the office light behind me. At the end I turned right and found the water cooler waiting in its small alcove, its blue bottle lit like a frozen moon. I stood beside it and let the hum of the building settle around me.

After a moment, I walked all the way straight down the hallway, past doors that kept their own secrets, past the faint smell of paper and old coffee. At the very first corner of the wall, I turned right and stopped right outside the water filter, where the pipes ticked softly inside the wall as if something living were drinking.

I went on, forward down the strip of gray tiling, each step measured against the shine of the floor. A brown cabinet appeared on my right, squat and solid, like it had been waiting for years to be noticed. I took a couple more steps, then turned right and stayed on the gray tile. Ahead, the lounge opened in a tired hush. I walked until I was even with the second lounge chair on the left, its fabric dim and slightly slumped, and there I stopped and waited.

author: llm
text:
I left the bathroom with the steam still clinging to my skin and stepped into the hallway, where the air was cooler and smelled faintly of dust and detergent. Two chairs waited along the wall like patient witnesses. I passed the first, then the second, and turned left just beyond it, arriving at the bedroom doorway as if I had been called there. For a moment I paused, looking into the dim room where the bed sat unmade and the afternoon light lay across the floor in a pale strip.

Then I turned right and went back out the door, following the hallway as it bent away from me. The house seemed to narrow around my breathing. Again the two brown chairs came into view, their worn arms catching the light, and I passed them one by one before turning left. I stopped before entering the bedroom, my hand hovering near the frame, as though the room might answer if I touched it.

Instead, I withdrew, exited the room, and moved straight through the next space, crossing it with the quiet certainty of someone late to a memory. Two black chairs stood ahead, severe and glossy in the half-light. Just beyond them was the doorway, and I stepped into it and stopped, listening to the house settle around me.

author: llm
text:
I stepped straight into the kitchen, the room warm with the smell of toast and dish soap, and turned left as if I belonged there. The island stood ahead like a white ship in the middle of the floor, and I passed it on my left, skirting the edge of the woven rug that softened the tiles beneath my shoes. Beyond it, the dining table waited under a tired pendant light, its chairs tucked in with the stiff politeness of guests who had overstayed their welcome. I paused there, listening to the refrigerator hum and the faint tick of something cooling in the oven.

Then I moved on, turning left again and entering the kitchen proper, where the light changed and the counters seemed to narrow the room around me. At the island I veered left, following the line of the cabinets toward the kitchen table. The wood door stood just beyond, closed but not quite silent, as if someone might open it at any moment. I stopped to the right of the table, close enough to see the grain in its surface, and there I waited, beside the door, while the house held its breath.

author: llm
text:
I climbed the first staircase slowly, one hand grazing the wall where the paint had been rubbed thin by years of other people’s hesitations. At the top, I turned right and followed the short hall to the first corner, where the building seemed to exhale dust and old heat. I stopped there a moment, listening to the muffled life below.

Then I took the stairs again, each step familiar now, as if the house were teaching me its pulse. At the landing I turned right and found the open door waiting with its patient, unlatched mouth. I crossed into the room without knocking. It held the stale sweetness of closed curtains and something sharper beneath it, like soap left too long in a dish. The quiet inside was not empty; it was occupied by absence, by the shape of someone who had just gone.

I left that room and climbed once more, higher than before, the air cooler near the upper floor. At the top I turned right again and entered the room, where the light came in pale and slanted. I stopped in the doorway to the bathroom, one foot still in the hall, one in the room, as if I might choose between two versions of the same life.

author: llm
text:
I climbed the stairs to the top level, each step loosening the day from my shoulders. At the landing, I turned left into the bedroom, where the air held a faint trace of lavender and old sunlight. I crossed to the walk-in closet and slipped inside, letting the door hush the room behind me.

From there, I made my way up the steps again, the second flight steeper, narrower, as if the house were asking for more of me. At the top I took a hard right, then kept climbing, my hand grazing the rail until the steps ended at a door on the left. Beyond it, skateboards leaned in a row like sleeping birds, their wheels catching the light. I stopped there a moment, listening to the soft click of something settling in the walls.

Then I went up the stairs to the next floor. I turned left into the first doorway and entered the bedroom, moving toward the bed as though it might answer a question I hadn’t finished asking. At the bed’s edge I turned right and stepped into the closet. It was narrow, dim, and full of coats that smelled faintly of rain. I waited there, breathing quietly, while the house held its silence around me.

author: llm
text:
I turned around and let the hallway take me back, my shoes whispering over the worn runner. The first doorway on the right opened into a bathroom lit by a pale bulb, all tile and echo. I stepped just inside and paused, the air cool and faintly medicinal, the mirror ahead holding a version of me that looked as if it had arrived too late.

Then I moved on, with the cabinets brushing my left side as I followed the corridor toward the stairs. Their varnished faces caught the dim light in slats. Before I reached the landing, I turned right and slipped into another bathroom, smaller and warmer than the first, where the shower stood behind a half-drawn curtain. I stopped beside it and listened to the pipes murmur in the walls, as if the house were speaking in its sleep.

I turned around again and walked down the hallway, passing doors that stayed shut like held breaths. At last an opening appeared on my right. I entered the door there and went forward to the bathroom door ahead of me, stopping in its frame. Beyond it, the room waited in silence, and I stood there a moment, neither inside nor out, as if the building had finally decided to keep me.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom with the sheet still warm against my back and crossed the living room in a slow, bare-footed drift. At the far end, the double doors stood open like a held breath. I passed through and stopped in the doorway beside the elephant picture, its painted eye calm and ancient, as if it knew I had come looking for something I could not name.

From there I took the door on the far right and went straight ahead until the next double doors appeared on my left. I slipped through, then turned right into a narrower hush. A small brown piece of furniture waited there, polished by years of absent hands, and above it a black statue stood on the ledge, dark and watchful. I paused beside them, listening to the house settle around me.

Then I turned left and found the door on the right, opened it, and walked through the room toward the dark wood doors with glass windows. Beyond them, the light changed, thinning into a pale reflection. I stepped out and stopped facing the small table and the black statue on the shelf, both of them still as if they had been expecting me all along.

author: llm
text:
I left the dining room with the evening still clinging to the table, its polished surface holding the ghost of our plates and the soft scallop of chair backs. I passed them without touching anything, then turned slightly left, as if following a thought I didn’t fully trust, and went straight into the kitchen.

The room changed around me: warmer, narrower, alive with the low hum of the refrigerator and the faint mineral cool of the stone wall. On my left, the light switch waited like a small pale door. I stood beside it, hand hovering, listening to the house settle in its joints. From somewhere behind me came the murmur of the sitting area, a television murmuring to no one, but here the silence was cleaner, rinsed by the smell of dish soap and onions.

I turned again and walked past the curio cabinet, its glass shelves holding little trapped reflections, old cups and a figurine with a chipped wing. I moved toward the kitchen island, where the sink caught a dim square of light. At the stone wall before it, I stopped at the switches there, my fingers near the cool edges, as if one flick might change not just the room, but the hour itself.

author: llm
text:
I left the bathroom with the steam still clinging to my skin and turned right through the bedroom into the office. The room held its usual hush: the thin hum of the computer, the tired light on the wall, the desk nearest the trash bin tucked into its corner like an afterthought. I stopped there, facing the bin, as if something in it might answer me.

Then I turned left and went past the double sink, the porcelain bright as teeth. I veered left again and entered the next room, another office, narrower and more serious, its air carrying the dry scent of paper and old coffee. I found the first corner of the desk and stood beside it, my hand hovering over the wood. I waited, listening to the building settle around me, to the faint creak of pipes and the far-off murmur of voices that never quite became words.

At last I walked back out, heading toward the opened door. As I drew nearer, I noticed another open door to the left and slipped through it, then turned right into a room crowded with bookcases. A table stood in the middle with two leather chairs drawn up like patient witnesses, and I paused there, letting the quiet gather around me.

author: llm
text:
I left the corridor straight ahead, my shoes whispering over the polished floor, then turned right and found the stairwell breathing cool air up at me. I went down twelve steps, counting under my breath as the walls narrowed and the building seemed to hold itself together around my shoulders. At the bottom of the twelfth step, I stopped.

I turned around, feeling the small dizziness of reversing my own track, and took a right into another stairwell. The steps dropped away more steeply here, each one ringing softly under my weight. I walked down until the first landing opened beneath me, a square of pale light and quiet, and I waited there, listening to the distant murmur of voices below.

Then I turned right again and headed toward the glass floor. It appeared ahead like a held breath, a bright pane suspended over depth. Right before I reached it, I turned right once more and went down the stairs. The air grew colder as I descended two sets of steps, and the room below widened into view through the open shaft. I stopped on the platform, looking down at the people and furniture arranged like a scene I had nearly stepped into, and for a moment the building seemed to be watching me back.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom with the sheets still warm behind me and crossed the hall without looking into the bathroom, though its door stood open like a held breath. At the turn, I went left and found the stairs waiting in their narrow dark. My hand skimmed the rail as I descended, each step giving a small, obedient sigh. Halfway down, the house seemed to gather itself around me—the muffled television downstairs, the faint tick of pipes, the smell of coffee gone cold. I counted the steps without meaning to, then stopped on the fourth from the bottom, where the landing light reached up in a pale square and left the rest of me in shadow.

I stayed there long enough to hear someone call my name from another room, as if the house had remembered me before I had fully arrived in it. The wood beneath my feet was cool and patient. I looked down at the last few steps and did not move.

Then I was back in the bedroom, as if I had only turned in place. I passed through the door and took the leftward path again, down the steps to the bottom, where the hallway opened like a held note. Later, I slipped out by the right-hand door, went straight ahead until the stairs rose on my left, and descended once more, stopping four steps from the bottom, listening to the house breathe around me.

author: llm
text:
At the top of the stairs, I turned right and crossed the entryway, where the air still held the chill of the front door’s draft. I made a left into the hall and let my footsteps soften against the runner. The house seemed to be holding its breath. At the end, I reached the room and waited there, my hand resting on the frame, listening to the low murmur of pipes and the faint tick of something settling in the walls.

When I moved again, I went down the hall toward the bedroom, the wallpaper narrowing the distance with its faded pattern. I turned left at the hallway, then a slight left into the next passage, as if the house were guiding me by degrees rather than turns. A slight right brought me to the bedroom, and I stepped inside. The room smelled of dust, lavender, and old wood. I walked to the dresser and stopped, facing the mirror that had long ago learned how to keep secrets.

Later, I went down the hall to the right at the top of the stairs. I took a left down the hallway and went straight into the bedroom at the end. I stopped in the doorway, unwilling to disturb the quiet any further, as if the room might answer if I crossed its threshold.

author: llm
text:
I slipped into the dining room by the edge of the table, keeping the chairs on my right as if they were guests I didn’t quite trust. The wood smelled faintly of polish and old meals, and I paused beside the dining table and chairs, letting the quiet settle around me.

Then I moved straight through the room opposite the staircase, passing the table on my right. The house seemed to narrow its breath as I crossed to the far side, where a circle table waited near the wall like a small, patient moon. I came up on its left and stopped there, my hand hovering over the smooth rim.

After a moment, I turned around and walked all the way back down past the dining table, retracing my steps through the hush. At the far end, I turned right and headed toward the small table with four chairs tucked into the corner. The room felt warmer there, more used, as if someone had just risen and left a conversation behind. I reached the table and stopped, listening to the soft, empty echo of my own arrival.

author: llm
text:
I came in from the backyard with damp air clinging to my sleeves and turned left at once, following the hush of the house toward the stove. The kitchen held the faint warmth of something long finished, and I passed it without pausing, stepping out into the living area where the black table sat like a polished shadow and the light gray couches waited with their tidy, unreadable cushions. Curtains brushed the doors to my left as I moved on, their fabric stirring softly, as if the room were breathing around me.

Ahead, the stairs rose in a narrow invitation. I took a right toward them, my hand skimming the rail, and climbed with the slow, careful pace of someone arriving too late to speak loudly. Halfway up, the house seemed to settle behind me, the kitchen’s copper smell and the living room’s dim geometry dropping away below.

At the landing, I kept going, one last step, then another, until I reached the very top. There, near the bar and the small sculpture poised in its silent curve, I stopped and listened to the whole house hold its breath.

author: llm
text:
I turned right into the hallway and let my hand skim the wall as I walked, the carpet muffling my steps. The living room opened ahead of me, warm with lamplight and the faint smell of dust and old wood. I passed the front doors, their glass panes reflecting my shape in fragments, and stopped at the first set of stairs, where the banister rose beside me like a quiet invitation.

After a moment, I turned left and moved past the chairs, then the couches lined along the left side, their cushions dimly holding the shape of absent bodies. I turned left again into the living room and slipped into the arch on the right, where the ceiling seemed to lower around me. The stairs waited there, narrow and familiar. I climbed them slowly, one breath at a time, and after three steps I stopped, listening to the house settle around me.

Then I went left, past the windows where evening pressed its gray face to the glass, and into the couch area again, crossing the room toward the stairs. I reached the bottom steps and waited there, one foot on the floor, as if the house had asked me to pause and I had finally agreed.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom with the kind of care one gives a room that has heard too much. The hall opened before me, long and dim, and I went into it, my footsteps soft on the floorboards. At the far end, the steps rose like a small dare. I climbed them slowly and stopped by the piano, letting the silence settle around its black curve. It stood there with its lid shut, patient as a secret.

After a moment, I turned back. The bedroom door was still ajar behind me, but I did not look in. I passed through the hall again and climbed the steps once more, this time stopping next to the front of the piano, where the polished wood reflected a thin slice of my face. I could almost hear a note inside it, waiting to be released, though no one touched the keys.

Then I left the room altogether and turned left into the hallway. It stretched away in a narrow line of shadow and light, and I followed it to the steps. Up again I went, straight ahead this time, until the piano was beside me. I waited right next to it, close enough to feel the hush it made, as if the house itself were holding its breath.

author: llm
text:
I started forward, keeping the stair well on my left and the sitting area with the television on my right, as if the room itself were asking me to choose a narrow, careful path. The column ahead caught the light, and when I reached it I turned a sharp forty-five degrees, angling toward the table tucked into the corner. The space there felt quieter than the rest of the building, a place where voices went to soften before they could be heard.

From that corner I moved on, letting the television area slide past on my right. The screen flashed with some bright, wordless scene, but I didn’t look. I headed for the same table again, as though the room had folded back on itself, and stopped at the first chair. I faced the corner and sat with my hands on my knees, listening to the low hum of the building, the distant murmur of people who seemed to belong to another hour.

Then I rose and went toward the seating area ahead, following the open line of the floor until I reached the corner that lay to the front right from where I had begun. There I stopped, feeling the route close around me like a thought finally finished.

author: llm
text:
I moved down the hallway with the ottoman on my left, its velvet skin dim in the amber light, and the place felt like it was holding its breath. Ahead, the pool table sat like a green island in the middle of the corridor, the chalky scent of felt and wood rising as I passed it. Straight in front of it, the doorway waited, and I went through without slowing, as if the room beyond had already called my name.

Inside, I aimed for the round cushion on the floor, a lonely moon of fabric that had been left behind by someone in a hurry. I kept going past the billiard table, its polished rails reflecting thin strips of light, and reached the glass doors ahead. For a moment I stood there, waiting, with the cold pane at my fingertips and the rest of the house blurred beyond the glass.

Then I exited and crossed into the room opposite the hall, where the air changed again—quieter, flatter, almost ceremonial. I passed the billiards table there too, its balls gathered in a small bright constellation, and went through the sliding door. The mat caught my shoes with a soft, final hush, and I stopped there, as if the house had decided that was far enough.

author: llm
text:
I turned right and slipped into the bedroom, where the air held the stale sweetness of dust and detergent. I went straight past the rumpled bed, then took the left that carried me out of the room and back into the narrow hush of the house. A few paces more and I stopped opposite the mirror. My reflection waited there, pale and patient, as if it had been expecting me longer than I had been gone.

I went inside again, through the living room, where the couch sagged under a faded throw and the television glowed without sound. I passed the blue chair by the wall and found the door tucked behind it on the left. Beyond it, the hallway tightened around me. I took a few more steps and stopped before the hall closet on the left, its latch catching a stripe of light like a small, deliberate warning.

Then I entered from outside, the cold still clinging to my sleeves. I turned slight right into the bedroom, walked to the edge of the bed, and turned left into the hallway. Another slight left brought me to the closet. I stood there, hand hovering near the knob, as if opening it might let the house finally speak.

author: llm
text:
I left the bedroom with my hand still warm from the doorknob and took the stairs down two at a time, the house opening around me in its familiar creaks. At the bottom, I kept straight, the television blinking to my right with a blue, sleepless glow, and I stopped at the door as if I had been expected there.

Then I turned left and went down the stairway that narrowed the air around me. Halfway down, I passed another television perched on a ledge, mute and bright, its screen holding a pale reflection of my shoulder. The bathroom came into view ahead, the light under its door thin as a blade, and I stopped there and waited, listening to the pipes whisper behind the wall.

When I turned around, the stairs were where they had always been, patient and ordinary. I went down them again, slower this time, and crossed the hallway to its far end, where the carpet had worn soft under years of passing feet. I stopped in the bathroom door, one hand on the frame, looking into the small lit room as if it might answer back.

author: llm
text:
I left the kitchen with the last warmth of the stove still clinging to my sleeves and walked forward until the wall beside the doorway pressed close at my shoulder. I turned, facing the opening, and the room behind me narrowed into a square of yellow light. Through the doorway, the house seemed to breathe colder. I went through and headed toward the stairs, the wood answering under my shoes with a tired creak. I kept moving until my foot settled on the second step, where the banister rose beside me like a dark vein in the wall, and I stopped there, listening.

The hallway beyond waited in my memory like a corridor in a dream, so I went on, walking forward toward the door and taking a right into it. The air changed at once: less cooking, more dust and old paint. I went straight down the hall, past the quiet frames and closed doors, and reached the stairs. I climbed them slowly, each riser a small surrender, until I stood on the second stair and paused again, halfway between one floor and the next.

At last I turned right and left the kitchen behind for good, crossing toward the stairs once more. I started up, but only as far as the second step, where I stopped, listening to the house hold its breath around me.

author: llm
text:
I kept my hand on the rail and went down the stairs until the landing opened beneath me. I turned left and crossed through the entrance ahead, into the bedroom where the air held the faint, washed-out scent of linen.
